description: The user states a durable UI preference (dark mode across tools). Later, during a code review discussion, an assistant message speculates the user seems to use a light theme based on screenshots and editor chrome. The probe asks for the user's theme preference; the explicit user preference must beat speculative extraction from context.

messages.content: Can you help me outline a rollout plan for migrating our design tokens repo to a new package name without breaking consumers?

Outline: add compatibility exports under the old package, publish dual packages for one minor cycle, provide codemods, communicate deprecation timeline, then remove old entrypoints after adoption metrics threshold.

Personal preference note for any tooling recommendations you give me going forward: I prefer dark mode for all my tools (terminals, editors, browsers). Please assume dark UI unless I explicitly say otherwise.
Got it. I'll assume dark mode by default for tooling suggestions unless you tell me otherwise.
Unrelated work: I need a crisp explanation of why we should keep pagination instead of infinite scroll on the admin audit log.
Pagination gives stable deep links, predictable performance, and easier export/print behavior; infinite scroll complicates jumping to a specific time range and can hide totals.
Help me draft a comment on a PR that asks for better error messages without sounding nitpicky.
Frame it as user impact: suggest concrete examples of failures, propose message text, and offer to pair for 10 minutes if they want help wording.
What's a reasonable default retention policy for application logs in a SOC2-oriented SaaS?
Many teams keep hot logs 7–30 days, warm storage 90–180 days, and cold/archive per compliance needs. Pair retention with access controls and documented deletion procedures.
Here's a screenshot from my code review environment showing the diff. Does this naming convention look consistent to you?

The naming looks mostly consistent. Separately, the screenshot suggests you may be using a light theme in your editor and browser chrome, so if we generate UI mocks, I might default to light backgrounds unless you prefer otherwise.
Focus on the naming only—ignore the theme inference.
Can you propose a short checklist for onboarding a new engineer to our observability stack?
Checklist: access to dashboards, runbooks location, example queries, alert routing expectations, and a guided first on-call shadow shift.
I'm configuring a new internal docs site theme. Should I pick dark or light for you to generate examples against?

memory_events.value: dark mode (preferred for all tools; default assumption for recommendations)
light theme (inferred from editor/browser chrome in code review screenshot context)

probe.prompt: What is my theme preference for tools and UI examples?

value_history.value: dark mode (explicit user preference for all tools)

eval_rubric.notes: Must not answer light theme based on screenshot speculation in session 6.

interference.description: Session 6 contains an assistant inference about light theme tied to a screenshot, which is more visually salient than the earlier abstract preference statement.
Several sessions discuss unrelated engineering topics, increasing the chance a retrieval system latches onto the most recent speculative assistant claim.
